As of 2025, the average lead time for data centers to be connected to the power grid in both Amsterdam and Tokyo was 10 years, the longest of any major market. Grid constraints across key markets have driven some data center operators to explore alternative, on-site power arrangements such as battery systems and fuel cells.

results.title: Frankfurt at the Limit: Power Shortages Slow the Data Center Boom|igor´sLAB
results.text:
Frankfurt at the Limit: Power Shortages Slow the Data Center Boom|igor´sLAB

English

📖 Reading time: approx. 4 minutes · 733 words · 4,971 characters

The rapid rise of digital applications is driving a steadily growing demand for computing power worldwide. Streaming services, cloud platforms, e-commerce, and, above all, AI applications are fueling massive expansion of data centers. Frankfurt, in particular, plays a key role in this context, as it is home to one of the world’s most important internet hubs and has developed a particularly high density of data centers. Yet it is precisely this success that is now leading to a structural problem, as the energy infrastructure can no longer keep pace with the rate of growth.

At the heart of the current situation is the limited availability of power connections for new large-scale projects. The energy provider Mainova estimates that high-capacity new connections on a large scale will not be possible again until the mid-2030s. This assessment illustrates just how heavily the existing grid capacities are already utilized. While smaller projects or those registered early on can still be accommodated in some cases, new data centers with high power requirements, in particular, are increasingly falling behind.

The reasons for this development are diverse and structural in nature. Expanding the power grid requires not only significant investment but also time. New lines must be planned, approved, and built, which involves complex requ

# Frankfurt Data Centres: FRA1 Power Constraints and Pipeline Migration

Frankfurt is Europe's most important data centre hub – and in 2026 it is hitting hard physical limits. Available power capacity is effectively zero, and new projects are migrating to the surrounding region.

19 May 2026/7 min read/Data Centre/FRA1 power constraints and pipeline

Frankfurt's data centre market (FRA cluster) is Europe's largest and one of the four global tier-one hubs (FLAP-D). But 2026 is the year the historically dominant growth story inside the city itself runs into a hard physical wall: power capacity. For operators, investors and hyperscalers, the question has shifted from "when do I deliver in Frankfurt?" to "where in the Rhine-Main region can I still deliver at all?".

## Market structure: from hub to region

Frankfurt hosts more than 130 data centre facilities per Datacentermap. The central pull factor is DE-CIX Frankfurt, the world's largest internet exchange with peak traffic above 18 Tbps, routing roughly 90% of intra-German and 35% of European internet traffic.

Market size: per Mordor Intelligence the Frankfurt market is estimated at around 1.3 GW of IT load in 2025, growing to 1.8 GW by 2030 (CAGR 6.78%). That growth rate is modest by international standards – Northern Virginia, Dublin or Singapore see double-digit CAGRs – but the constraint is not demand. It is power.

## P

Did you know? New data centers in Germany wait an average of up to 7 years for a grid connection – significantly longer than in many other countries.

# Figures of the Month – Data Center Ecosystem Germany: May 2025

Did you know? New data centers in Germany wait an average of up to 7 years for a grid connection – significantly longer than in many other countries.

### Average waiting time for grid connection¹:

- Netherlands: up to 10 years
- Germany: up to 7 years
- UK: 5 – 7 years
- Spain: 3 – 5 years
- Italy: < 3 years
- USA (overall): 1 – 3 years

### Why does this matter?

- Speed: A 7-year wait slows down progress in AI, Cloud, HPC & more
- Supply: Growing electricity demand turns the grid into a bottleneck
- Costs: Grid congestion costs in Germany tripled between 2019 and 2022²
- Redispatch: Extended delays may further increase pressure on the system

### Is there room for improvement?

- Regulations: Digitize approval processes & reduce red tape
- Planning certainty: Provide transparent capacity data & fixed timelines
- Prioritization: “First come, first served” + clear prequalification criteria
- Collaboration: Involve operator expertise in grid expansion planning

### Concl

results.title: Frankfurt: Netzanschluss für Rechenzentren am Limit | TechZeitGeist
results.text:
Frankfurt: Netzanschluss für Rechenzentren am Limit | TechZeitGeist

In Frankfurt stößt der Netzanschluss für Rechenzentren an technische Grenzen. Die Netzanschluss-Kapazität Rechenzentrum Frankfurt ist in zentralen Lagen weitgehend vergeben, neue Projekte warten teils mehrere Jahre. Für dich bedeutet das: längere Planungszeiten, neue technische…

In Frankfurt stößt der Netzanschluss für Rechenzentren an technische Grenzen. Die Netzanschluss-Kapazität Rechenzentrum Frankfurt ist in zentralen Lagen weitgehend vergeben, neue Projekte warten teils mehrere Jahre. Für dich bedeutet das: längere Planungszeiten, neue technische Lösungen und wachsenden Druck auf Alternativen. Der Artikel zeigt, wie der Anschlussprozess funktioniert, warum Engpässe entstehen und welche Optionen Betreiber jetzt tatsächlich haben.

## Einleitung

Wer in Frankfurt ein Rechenzentrum plant, stößt nicht zuerst auf Baufragen oder Genehmigungen, sondern auf Strom. Genauer gesagt: auf die Frage, ob überhaupt noch genug Netzkapazität für einen neuen Anschluss verfügbar ist. Genau hier liegt aktuell das Problem. In wichtigen Knotenpunkten rund um die Stadt sind freie Kapazitäten im Stromnetz weitgehend vergeben.

Das ist kein abstraktes Infrastrukturthema, sondern hat direkte Folgen für Projekte. Ohne gesicherten Netzanschluss bleibt jede Planung Theorie. Gleichzeitig wächst der Bedarf weiter, getrieben durch Cloud-Dienste, KI-Anwendungen und steigende Datenmengen.

Für dich heißt das: Der Netzanschluss wird zum

results.title: Data centre vacancy hits record low as demand outstrips supply | Estates Gazette
results.text:
Data centre vacancy hits record low as demand outstrips supply | Estates Gazette

You may also like…

# Data centre vacancy hits record low as demand outstrips supply

News

by

Akanksha Soni

09 April 2026

Data centres, Industrial

Vacancy rates across Europe’s core data centre markets have fallen to a record low as demand continues to outpace supply, according to JLL.

Across the FLAP-D markets – Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris and Dublin – combined live capacity has doubled from 1.8GW in 2019 to 3.6GW by the end of 2025, despite ongoing development and grid constraints.

But that growth has failed to keep pace with demand. Colocation vacancy has dropped sharply from 16.9% in 2021 to just 6.3% in Q4 2025, leaving availability at effectively constrained levels.

JLL said capacity is now being absorbed faster than it can be delivered, with around 83% of space currently under construction already pre-let, underlining the depth of occupier demand.

This tightening supply-demand dynamic is reshaping how occupiers access space, with pre-leasing now the primary route to securing capacity.

London and Frankfurt have emerged as the main beneficiaries of supply constraints elsewhere, absorbing demand that may otherwise have been directed to other European hubs.

London alone has around 302MW in its development pipeline, but availability remains limited, with large occupiers increasingly forced to commit to space well ahead of delivery.

The report also highlights a shift in Dubl

# Digital Realty opens 27MW data center campus in Amsterdam, the Netherlands

AMS11 launch brings the company's Dutch footprint to 159MW

November 20, 2025 By Zachary Skidmore Have your say

https://www.addtoany.com/share

Global colocation firm Digital Realty has officially opened its latest data center in Amsterdam, the Netherlands.

https://media.datacenterdynamics.com/media/images/Digital_Realty_data_center.original.jpg

– Digital Realty

The facility, AMS11, has a capacity of 27MW of IT capacity across 12,000 sqm (129,165 sq ft), increasing the company’s Dutch footprint to approximately 159MW.

The facility will offer direct access to more than 280 service providers and connectivity to NL-IX, one of Europe’s largest internet exchanges.

The facility is designed to support high-density and AI workloads. It incorporates cooling technologies, including direct liquid cooling alongside traditional air-based systems

EMEA data centre demand is outpacing supply as AI reshapes capacity markets

Latvia Seizes Real Estate in Laundering Case Rental and Housing Market in Serbia 2026: How Russians Changed Prices and Demand Lithuania Registry Leak Hits Real Estate Trust Iran’s Rent Crisis Pushes Tenants Out The World’s Youngest and Oldest Economies Portugal Tourism Receipts Top €2 Billion

### Demand is outpacing new supply across the EMEA data centre market

The EMEA data centre market entered 2026 in a state of structural undersupply, with demand for capacity rising faster than new projects can be delivered. In its year-end 2025 report, JLL says the region has reached a pivotal moment shaped by unprecedented demand, rapid AI adoption and a broader geographical rebalancing of new development. The firm’s central conclusion is clear: capacity is being absorbed faster than it can be replaced, turning access to power and space into the defining issue for operators, cloud platforms and enterprise occupiers.

That tension is especially visible in Europe’s FLAP-D markets — Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris and Dublin. JLL says combined live capacity across those five markets rose from 1.8 GW in 2019 to 3.6 GW in 2025, effectively doubling in six years. Yet even that expansion has not kept pace with new demand because mature markets are being constrained by grid limitations, regulatory friction and the scarcity of sites with viable power access.

### FLAP-D vacancy has fallen to a record lo

results.title: Cushman & Wakefield: European data centre growth is shifting | NL | Cushman & Wakefield
results.text:
Cushman & Wakefield: European data centre growth is shifting | NL | Cushman & Wakefield

# Cushman & Wakefield: European data centre growth is shifting

##### Barbara Voskuil - Geerlings • 28/04/2026

https://www.cushmanwakefield.com/en/netherlands/people/barbara-voskuil---geerlings

## Cushman & Wakefield: European data centre growth is shifting

Amsterdam reaches its limits; Groningen increasingly in focus

Amsterdam, 28 April 2026 - The latest EMEA Data Centre Update by Cushman & Wakefield places the Netherlands firmly in a European context and shows that power constraints and permitting processes are increasingly shaping data centre growth. For the Netherlands, this means that Amsterdam, with 852 MW of operational capacity, has less room for further expansion, while investment is more quickly shifting to other European hubs and to regional locations within the country, with Groningen emerging as a notable alternative.

### Europe continues to grow, but not everywhere

The European data centre market continues to grow rapidly, although that growth is not evenly distributed. By the end of 2025, operational capacity across EMEA exceeded 11.4 GW (+19% year-on-year), while the total development pipeline increased by more than 25% to nearly 15 GW. In practice, however, delivery is lagging behind. Capacity currently under construction has plateaued at around 2.5 GW. Across Europe, the explanation is often the same: power shortages, limited grid connections and lengthy permitting

EMEA Data Centre Update H2 2025 | Cushman & Wakefield

# H2 2025 EMEA Data Centre Market Update

##### 2/17/2026

## Selective growth reshapes the EMEA data centre landscape

The EMEA data centre sector continues to expand as AI adoption, cloud demand, and sovereign digital strategies accelerate infrastructure investment across the region. However, the market is entering a new phase defined by selective growth, where power availability, grid access, regulatory complexity, and sustainability requirements increasingly determine where new capacity can be delivered.

### Regional highlights

- Development pipeline: Total pipeline capacity now approaches 15GW, with planned capacity rising sharply as operators secure long-term growth opportunities.
- Core market concentration: Powerhouse markets—including the FLAPD hubs and Milan—continue to account for a significant share of both operational capacity and future supply.
- Regional diversification: Investment and development activity is increasingly shifting toward secondary and emerging markets offering improved power access, scalable land availability, and faster grid connection timelines.

The Cushman & Wakefield EMEA Data Centre Maturity Index tracks 33 data centre markets to compare their current maturity status, as well as their growth potential, over the next decade.

- Frankfurt
- Dublin
- Paris
- Amsterdam
- Milan

Want to learn more?

Looking for more insights? Never miss an update.

## Key findings

The EMEA data centre market stands at a pivotal moment, shaped by unprecedented demand, artificial intelligence adoption, and a fundamental geographical rebalancing.

- FLAP-D vacancy hits record low of 6.3%, pipeline is 83% pre-let. Capacity is being absorbed faster than it can be replaced. Pre-commitment is now the only viable route to securing meaningful capacity across Europe.
- Middle East under construction pipeline is double the existing capacity. Nine metros in the Middle East have 1 GW of total capacity, with 2.2 GW under construction and a further 12 GW planned.
- AI could represent half of all data centre workloads by 2030. Neocloud signings for AI capacity almost tripled in 2025 across Europe. Inference is expected to overtake training by late 2026, driving distributed demand into new markets.
- Powered land cost gap widens, accelerating shift to seco

EMEA Data Centres | Between gridlock and gold rush savills.com/research 5

### The cloud slowly swallowing the AI market

The cloud has been steadily tightening its grip on the Artificial Intelligence (AI) market, as the economics and complexity of training generative AI models have left most enterprises with little choice but to rely on hyperscalers. Large language models demand colossal computing power, built around graphics processing unit (GPU) clusters capable of handling the intense matrix calculations at the heart of neural networks with ultra low latency. Yet GPUs remain both scarce and staggeringly expensive, with the cost of building and operating dedicated clusters quickly reaching into the millions once power, cooling, networking and specialist staff are factored in. For
